title: मार्कण्डेय स्तुति
devanagariText:
॥ श्री मार्कण्डेय स्तुति ॥

श्रीमार्कण्डेय उवाच —

ॐ नमः शिवाय शान्ताय सर्वलोकमहेश्वर।
त्र्यम्बकाय नमस्तुभ्यं मृत्युञ्जय नमोऽस्तु ते॥ १॥

रुद्रं पशुपतिं स्थाणुं नीलकण्ठमुमापतिम्।
नमामि शिरसा देवं किं नो मृत्युः करिष्यति॥ २॥

नीलकण्ठं विरूपाक्षं विश्वरूपं महेश्वरम्।
विश्वनाथं विरूपं च विश्वेशं रभसं हरम्॥ ३॥

सर्वज्ञं सर्वगं शम्भुं सर्वलोकमहेश्वरम्।
सर्वसिद्धिप्रदं शान्तं ब्रह्मविष्णुमहेश्वरम्॥ ४॥

शूलपाणिं पिनाकेशं उमाकान्तं दिगम्बरम्।
वृषभध्वजमीशानं गङ्गाधरमनाशकम्॥ ५॥

त्रिलोचनं दशभुजं सर्वलोककृपापरम्।
कपालमालाधरं देवं कर्पूरगौरशोभितम्॥ ६॥

कालकालं कालरुद्रं कालाग्निं कालनाशनम्।
कालज्ञं कालमथनं कालचक्रप्रवर्तकम्॥ ७॥

मृकण्डुसुतरक्षार्थमागतं यमनाशनम्।
तं नमामि महादेवं मार्कण्डेयवरप्रदम्॥ ८॥

मृत्योर्मृत्युं नमाम्यहं सदाशिवमनामयम्।
यो मार्कण्डेयमुद्धृत्य कालमृत्युं व्यनाशयत्॥ ९॥

महाकालं महादेवं मृत्युञ्जयमनामयम्।
नमामि जगतां नाथं शिवमेकं सनातनम्॥ १०॥

॥ इति श्री मार्कण्डेय स्तुति सम्पूर्णा ॥
transliteration:
|| Shri Markandeya Stuti ||

Shri Markandeya Uvacha —

Om namah Shivaya shantaya sarva-loka-Maheshvara |
Tryambakaya namas-tubhyam Mrityunjaya namo'stu te || 1 ||

Rudram Pashupatim Sthanum Nilakantham Umapatim |
Namami shirasa devam kim no mrityuh karishyati || 2 ||

Nilakantham Virupaksham vishvarupam Maheshvaram |
Vishvanatham virupam cha vishvesham rabhasam Haram || 3 ||

Sarvajnam sarvagam Shambhum sarva-loka-Maheshvaram |
Sarva-siddhi-pradam shantam Brahma-Vishnu-Maheshvaram || 4 ||

Shulapanim Pinakesham Uma-kantam digambaram |
Vrishabha-dhvajam Ishanam Ganga-dharam anashakam || 5 ||

Trilochanam dasha-bhujam sarva-loka-kripa-param |
Kapala-mala-dharam devam karpura-gaura-shobhitam || 6 ||

Kala-kalam Kala-rudram Kala-agnim kala-nashanam |
Kala-jnam kala-mathanam kala-chakra-pravartakam || 7 ||

Mrikandu-suta-rakshar-tham-agatam Yama-nashanam |
Tam namami Mahadevam Markandeya-vara-pradam || 8 ||

Mrityor-mrityum namamy-aham Sada-Shivam-anamayam |
Yo Markandeyam-uddhritya Kala-mrityum vyanashayat || 9 ||

Mahakalam Mahadevam Mrityunjayam-anamayam |
Namami jagatam natham Shivam-ekam sanatanam || 10 ||

|| Iti Shri Markandeya Stuti Sampurna ||
hindiMeaning:
हे सर्वलोकमहेश्वर, शान्तस्वरूप शिव को नमस्कार! हे त्र्यम्बक, आपको नमस्कार! हे मृत्युञ्जय, आपको नमस्कार!॥ १॥

रुद्र, पशुपति, स्थाणु, नीलकण्ठ, उमापति — उन देव को मैं शिर झुकाकर नमन करता हूँ। मृत्यु हमारा क्या करेगी?॥ २॥

नीलकण्ठ, विरूपाक्ष, विश्वरूप, महेश्वर, विश्वनाथ, विरूप, विश्वेश, प्रचण्ड, हर — इन सबको नमन॥ ३॥

सर्वज्ञ, सर्वव्यापी, शम्भु, सर्वलोक-महेश्वर, सर्वसिद्धिप्रद, शान्त, ब्रह्मा-विष्णु-महेश्वर स्वरूप को नमन॥ ४॥

शूलपाणि, पिनाक धारी, उमाकान्त, दिगम्बर, वृषभध्वज, ईशान, गङ्गाधर, अविनाशी को नमन॥ ५॥

त्रिलोचन, दशभुज, सर्वलोक पर कृपापर, कपालमाला धारी, कर्पूरगौर शोभायमान देव को नमन॥ ६॥

काल के काल, काल-रुद्र, कालाग्नि, कालनाशक, कालज्ञ, कालमथन, कालचक्र प्रवर्तक को नमन॥ ७॥

मृकण्डु-पुत्र (मार्कण्डेय) की रक्षा के लिए आए, यम को नष्ट करने वाले, मार्कण्डेय को वर देने वाले महादेव को नमन॥ ८॥

मृत्यु की भी मृत्यु, सदाशिव, अनामय — जिन्होंने मार्कण्डेय को उद्धारकर कालमृत्यु का नाश किया — उन्हें मैं नमन करता हूँ॥ ९॥

महाकाल, महादेव, मृत्युञ्जय, अनामय — जगत् के नाथ, एकमात्र सनातन शिव को मैं नमन करता हूँ॥ १०॥
benefits: Invokes victory over untimely death (akaal mrityu nivaran)
Invokes the blessing of long life and health
Traditionally recited for removal of fear of death completely
Protects children from dangers and illness
description: Dedicated to Lord Shiva, the Markandeya Stuti (मार्कण्डेय स्तुति) is a devotional hymn of praise revered in Hindu tradition. Attributed to Markandeya Purana / Shiva Purana, this prayer holds a special place in Hindu devotional literature. Regular recitation is believed to remove fear of death completely, protect children from dangers and illness, and grant victory over untimely death (Akaal Mrityu Nivaran). This sacred text is ideally recited on Monday (Somvar) and during Maha Shivaratri and Pradosh for enhanced blessings. The text contains 10 verses and takes around 7 minutes to recite, fitting conveniently into a daily spiritual routine. On this page, you can read the complete Markandeya Stuti in Devanagari Sanskrit with English transliteration, Hindi meaning (arth), and free PDF download for offline recitation.
seoDescription: Read Markandeya Stuti in Sanskrit with transliteration and Hindi meaning. Child-sage Markandeya's prayer to Lord Shiva who conquered death.
alsoKnownAs: Sage Markandeya's Prayer to Lord Shiva
relatedSearches: Markandeya Stuti lyrics in Sanskrit Devanagari
Markandeya story Lord Shiva conquered Yama Kala
Mrikandu Rishi son Markandeya 16 years death
Markandeya Stuti for protection from untimely death
Markandeya Purana Shiva Stuti complete text
Markandeya Stuti for children health protection

seoFaqs.question: Who was Sage Markandeya and why did he compose this stuti?
seoFaqs.answer: Markandeya was the son of Sage Mrikandu, born after long austerities to the childless couple. The Puranas relate that Lord Shiva offered Mrikandu a choice — a noble but short-lived son who would die at sixteen, or a long-lived but mediocre son. Mrikandu chose the noble son, and Markandeya was born. As his sixteenth year approached, his parents revealed his fated death. The boy went to a Shiva temple, embraced the Shivalinga, and recited this stuti continuously. When Yama, the god of death, arrived to take his soul and threw his noose around the boy and the Linga together, Shiva burst forth from the Linga in his Mrityunjaya (conqueror of death) form, kicked Yama away, and granted Markandeya immortality at the age of sixteen forever. The stuti is therefore the very prayer that conquered death itself. (Sources: Linga Purana 1.30; Shiva Purana Kotirudra Samhita; Markandeya Purana introductory chapters.)

seoFaqs.question: Where is the Markandeya Stuti preserved in the scriptures?
seoFaqs.answer: Versions of the stuti are preserved across multiple Puranas: the Markandeya Purana (named after the sage himself, where he is the principal narrator), the Linga Purana (Purvabhaga 30 — the canonical Markandeya-Yama narrative), the Shiva Purana (Kotirudra Samhita and Vidyeshvara Samhita), the Skanda Purana (Kashi Khanda), and the Padma Purana. The version on this page draws from the Markandeya Purana and Shiva Purana traditions and contains ten verses focused on Shiva's epithets as Mrityunjaya, Kalakala, Mahakala, Tryambaka and Pashupati. The opening verse Om namah Shivaya shantaya sarva-loka-Maheshvara is also cited in the daily Shiva-archana of many lineages. (Sources: Linga Purana Purvabhaga 30; Shiva Purana Kotirudra Samhita; Markandeya Purana 1-7.)

seoFaqs.question: How is Markandeya Stuti related to the Mahamrityunjaya Mantra?
seoFaqs.answer: The Markandeya episode is the foundational mythology of the Mahamrityunjaya Mantra (Tryambakam yajamahe sugandhim pushti-vardhanam, urvarukam-iva bandhanat mrityor mukshiya ma'mritat — Rig Veda 7.59.12). Tradition holds that this mantra was the central beej revealed to Markandeya during his prayer, and that it has retained the power to grant victory over death ever since. The stuti and the mantra are therefore complementary: the stuti is the praise-prayer that frames the situation, while the mantra is the seed-formula at its heart. The verse Tryambakaya namastubhyam Mrityunjaya namo'stu te in the stuti directly echoes the mantra's address to the three-eyed lord. The combined recitation of stuti + mantra (108 or 1008 times) is the most powerful Shaiva remedy for fear of death and untimely fatality. (Sources: Rig Veda 7.59.12; Mahamrityunjaya Stotra collections.)

seoFaqs.question: What does the verse Kim no mrityuh karishyati mean?
seoFaqs.answer: Verse 2 — Rudram Pashupatim Sthanum Nilakantham Umapatim, namami shirasa devam kim no mrityuh karishyati — concludes with the bold declaration: I bow my head to Rudra, Pashupati, Sthanu, Nilakantha, Umapati — what indeed can death do to me? This is the philosophical core of the entire stuti: by surrendering completely to Mahadeva, the devotee enters the protection of the One who is himself the lord of death (Mahakala, Mrityunjaya), and death therefore loses all power over them. The line is one of the most famous fearless declarations in Sanskrit devotional literature, frequently quoted in Shaiva sermons and used as a protective mantra by ill or aged devotees seeking peace at the end of life. (Sources: Linga Purana 30 commentary; Shaiva Vedanta tradition.)

seoFaqs.question: What benefits does Markandeya Stuti grant to its reciter?
seoFaqs.answer: Tradition assigns the Markandeya Stuti the following specific benefits: victory over untimely death (akala-mrityu nivarana), the principal benefit; long life (dirgha-ayuh) and good health; protection of children from disease, accidents and untimely fatality (parents recite this for sick children); freedom from fear of death (mrityu-bhaya nashana) for the elderly and the chronically ill; relief from chronic illnesses, especially incurable ones; protection from poison, weapons, snake-bite, fire and water; courage and fearlessness in the face of crisis; and ultimately darshan of Mahadeva at the time of death. It is one of the principal Shaiva prayers recited during Maha Mrityunjaya Homa and during the puja of Tryambakeshwar Jyotirlinga. (Sources: Linga Purana phalashruti; Shaiva Maha-Mrityunjaya rituals.)

seoFaqs.question: When should Markandeya Stuti be recited?
seoFaqs.answer: The stuti is most powerfully recited on Monday (Somvar), the day sacred to Lord Shiva, and on Pradosh tithi (the trayodashi of every paksha — Shukla Pradosh and Krishna Pradosh — twice per month), at the Pradosh kala just before sunset. Major annual occasions include Maha Shivaratri (Phalguna Krishna Chaturdashi), Sawan month Mondays (Shravan Somvar), Karthik month Mondays, Aaradra Nakshatra days, every Krishna Chaturdashi, and the appearance day of the Mahamrityunjaya. It is also recited during illness, before surgery, during pregnancy complications, and at the bedside of the dying as part of the Shaiva antya-karma. The early morning sandhya (Brahma muhurta) and the Pradosh kala are the two most powerful times. (Sources: drikpanchang.com Pradosh; Shaiva nitya-puja paddhati.)

seoFaqs.question: What is the meaning of Mrikandu-suta-rakshartham agatam yama-nashanam?
seoFaqs.answer: Verse 8 — Mrikandu-suta-raksha-artham-agatam Yama-nashanam, tam namami Mahadevam Markandeya-vara-pradam — captures the entire mythology in a single shloka: I bow to Mahadeva who came to protect the son of Mrikandu (Markandeya), who destroyed Yama (the death-god), and who granted the boon to Markandeya. By referring to Shiva as Yama-nashana — the destroyer of Yama — the verse asserts a remarkable theological point: even Yama, who is otherwise irresistible to all beings, is subject to the supreme authority of Mahadeva. For the devotee facing fear of death, this is the assurance that no fate decreed by Yama can stand against the will of Shiva when invoked through this prayer. (Sources: Linga Purana Markandeya narrative; Shaiva Yama-vijay theology.)
